rulings.Case Title: HELLEN FERNANDEZ VS. GRUMA CORPORATION, A NEVADA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 1, 2025, line 1, PETITIONER HELLEN FERNANDEZ'S Amended PETITION TO VACATE ARBITRATION The petition brought by Hellen Fernandez to vacate an arbitration award arising out of her employment arbitration with respondent Gruma Corporation is dismissed as untimely served and filed. The petition was filed outside of the 100-day period to serve and file a petition to vacate the award. (CCP 1288 ["A petition to vacate an award or to correct an award shall be served and filed not later than 100 days after the date of the service of a signed copy of the award on the petitioner.".] Here, the award issued on December 6, 2024. (Fund Amended Dec., p. 193 [for convenience, the court will use pdf pagination of the electronic documents appearing in the court's register of actions].) The filing here was made on April 3, 2025, 118 days after the award's issuance. The method and date of service is not disclosed, and thus the precise date of Fernandez's deadline is not apparent from the papers, but the parties agree that the present petition was served and filed more than 100 days after service. Fernandez argues for equitable tolling, arguing that her petition was timely filed in Alameda County, which she erroneously believed was the proper venue, and excuses her failure to serve Gruma in the manner required for summons as a mistake. (See CCP 1290.4(b)(1).) The court does not find equitable tolling appropriate here; equitable tolling goes to reasons why the law should bend for fairness, not mistakes. (See Law Finance Group, LLC v. Key (2023) 14 Cal.5th 932, 954 ["equitable tolling is not a cure-all for an entirely common state of affairs but instead applies only in carefully considered situations to prevent the unjust technical forfeiture of causes of action"] [internal quotation marks and citations omitted].) Application of statute is not an unjust technical forfeiture. The petition is dismissed. The court denies Gruma's request for sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: YAN GROSSMAN VS. HIGH GROUND CONSTRUCTION, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Depositions Of High Ground Construction, Inc.'S Person(S) Most Knowledgeable And Jack Schnell, And Accompanying Document Production, Request For Monetary, Issue And Evidentiary Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Ronald F. Berestka And Exhibits A Through L
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 1, 2025, line 5, PLAINTIFF YAN GROSSMAN'S Motion To Compel Depositions Of High Ground Construction, Inc.'S Person(S) Most Knowledgeable And Jack Schnell, And Accompanying Document Production, Request For Monetary, Issue And Evidentiary Sanctions (TENATIVE RULING PART 1 OF 2) Plaintiff Yan Grossman's motion to compel depositions of High Ground Construction, Inc.'s person most qualified and of Jack Schnell, and for production of documents, and for monetary and evidentiary sanctions, is granted in part and denied in part. High Ground is a dissolved corporation. Schnell was its principal and is apparently the only individual who could give testimony on its behalf. Schnell suffered a major medical event while the case was pending and now suffers from dementia. (Zunker Dec. paras. 6, 9.) Respecting Schnell, the court rejects the argument that he was improperly served through High Ground's counsel; he is the only officer of High Ground. But the court accepts that he cannot be deposed and is not competent to testify. The court orders the conditional evidentiary sanction that he may not offer testimony at trial unless he recovers and submits to deposition by the discovery cutoff in this case. Respecting High Ground, the court orders the conditional evidentiary sanction that it may not offer testimony at trial on any matter covered in the list of PMQ topics attached as Exhibit I to the Berestka Declaration unless it identifies another deponent as PMQ who is qualified to provide testimony about the listed topics within 10 days of entry of this order and makes that PMQ available for deposition on a mutually agreed date within 25 days. Grossman also seeks an order for production of documents listed in the deposition notices attached as Exhibits H and I to the Berestka Declaration. The court denies relief; no separate statement was provided and the moving papers do not set forth specific facts showing good cause for production. (See Rule of Court 3.1345(a)(5); CCP 2025.450(b)(1).) Grossman also seeks monetary sanctions. In light of the evidentiary sanctions the court has ordered, and the circumstances that caused Schnell and High Ground to be unable to provide testimony, the court finds that imposition of monetary sanctions would be unjust and does not award them. (END OF TENATIVE RULING PART 1, SEE PART 2) = (301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: LAURA KIRKHAM VS. PERSADO INC., A CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Thursday, May 1, 2025, line 2, PLAINTIFF LAURA KIRKHAM'S Motion For Leave To File First Amended Complaint (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Plaintiff Laura Kirkham's motion for leave to amend her complaint is denied. Plaintiff seeks to add a breach of contract cause of action to allege that her former employer Persado breached its employment contract with her when it failed to pay her commission for a sales deal with Gap Inc. that Gap ultimately cancelled before paying Persado anything on the deal. The court denies leave to amend to add this new cause of action for two reasons. First, the motion is brought only two court days before trial. Plaintiff offers no explanation for her delay in taking discovery to learn the facts that gave rise to the proposed amendment. Second, the amendment would be futile. Plaintiff asserted the theory underlying her new breach of contract claim in opposition to Persado's summary adjudication motion as one basis for her claim that Persado breached the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The court has considered the claim and the evidentiary support for it in that context and has found insufficient evidence as a matter of law to create a triable issue of fact that Persado agreed to waive the 12-month requirement for sales deals in connection with Plaintiff's commission plan. (See Order, April 14, 2025, at pp. 8-9.) It would be futile to permit the amendment. (See Melican v. Regents of University of California (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 168, 176 [late and futile amendment properly denied].) To permit Kirkham to amend to assert a claim based on evidence that was rejected at summary adjudication two days before trial would unfairly deprive Persado of the opportunity to move for summary adjudication on the new claim. Plaintiff seeks to add a violation of Labor Code 2751 as a predicate to her Unfair Competition Law claim. This provision requires employers to set forth in writing the method by which commissions will be paid to employees. The court denies leave to amend because of Kirkham's delay in seeking to amend until just before trial. In any event, amendment would be futile. As discussed in greater detail in the April 14 order, the Gap deal was not eligible for any commission when Kirkham closed the deal because it had a term of less than 12 months, pursuant to Persado's written commission structure. Persado later created a new written commission structure for deals like the Gap deal. Kirkham was not eligible for commission on the Gap deal under either of written commission methods Persado promulgated. Finally, Kirkham seeks to add a punitive damages claim. Because the court has granted summary adjudication on her wrongful termination claim, and no other causes of action alleged in the complaint give rise to punitive damages, the court denies leave to add this prayer for relief. (end of tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) = (301/CVA)
